coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot integrated cerium tungstate nanocomposites for photocatalytic degradation and sensing applications

coredata.dc:creator: Paul, Anju
Tomy, Ann Mary
Joseph, Kuruvilla
Mathew, Beena
Kurian, Manju
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dot-modified cerium tungstate binary composites (CTCD) were synthesized through a hydrothermal synthesis followed by ultrasonication. Standard techniques investigated structural, optical, and morphological characteristics. The binary composites were successfully applied for the photocatalytic abatement of the organic dye methylene blue. Maximum photocatalytic performance was displayed by CT10CD. 85 % among the different weight percentage binary composites and exhibited a degradation efficiency greater than 85 %. Various parameters, such as catalyst dosage, pH, and dye concentration, significantly affected the composites' photocatalytic performance. The prepared binary composites were also effective in sensing trinitrophenol by quenching fluorescence via inner filter effect. These results underline the usage of cost-effective, easily attainable carbon dot-modified cerium tungstate binary nanocomposites as efficient sensors and photocatalysts for the degradation of pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cerium tungstate
Carbon dot
Nanocomposite
Methylene blue
Photocatalysis
Sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots from Jackfruit peel
Synthesis of carbon dot incorporated cerium tungstate

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of CT, CT5CD, CT10CD, CT20CD, CT30CD and CD.
a) FT-IR spectra of a CT, b CT5CD, c CT10CD, d CT20CD, e CT30CD, f CD, b) Raman spectra of CT, CT5CD, CT10CD, CT20CD, CT30CD, CD.
FESEM of a) CT, b) CT10CD.
Elemental mapping of CT10CD a)Ce b)W c)C d)O e)EDX mapping of CT10CD f) EDX spectrum of CT10CD.
a) TEM images of CD, b) CT10CD, c) SAED pattern of CT10CD.
a) XPS full survey spectrum of CT, deconvoluted spectra of b) W, c) O, d) Ce.
TGA curve(blue) along with their derivatives (DTG)(red), displaying thermal decomposition of CT.
a) UV-DRS spectrum of CT & CT10CD, b) Absorbance spectrum of CT10CD c) Tauc plot of CT, CT5CD, CT10CD, CT20CD and CT30CD.
Deconvoluted PL spectrum of a) CT, b) CT10CD.

a) Photoluminescence spectra of CD, CT, CT5CD, CT10CD, CT20CD and CT30CD b) PL spectra of CT10CD at different excitation wavelength, c) Selectivity study, d) Selectivity curve of 2NP, e) Selectivity curve of 4NP, f) Selectivity curve of TNP, g) plot of fluorescence response (I0/I) of CT10CD and the concentration of TNP from 100 to 1000 μM, h) Lifetime analysis, i) UV–vis. absorption spectra of TNP, CT10CD and excitation spectra of CT10CD, j) Zeta potential analysis.
Degradation of MB in the presence of a) CD, b) CT, c) CT5CD, d) CV10CD, e) CT20CD, f) CT30CD at pH 9, photocatalyst dosage 0.02 g and dye concentration 5 mg/L.
Degradation kinetics according to pseudo-first-order kinetics for CT10CD.
Effect of a) dye concentration, b) catalyst dose, c) pH d) catalyst on degradation.
HPLC analysis at initial and final stages of reaction.
a) XRD before and after catalysis, b) FT-IR before and after catalysis, c) degradation efficiency with time.
Schematic representation for the synthesis of carbon dot incorporated cerium tungstate.
Comparison with some reported fluorescent sensing of TNP.
Comparison with some reported photocatalytic degradation of MB.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot integrated cerium tungstate nanocomposites for photocatalytic degradation and sensing applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam, Kerala 686560, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dot-modified cerium tungstate binary composites (CTCD) were synthesized through a hydrothermal synthesis followed by ultrasonication. Standard techniques investigated structural, optical, and morphological characteristics. The binary composites were successfully applied for the photocatalytic abatement of the organic dye methylene blue. Maximum photocatalytic performance was displayed by CT10CD. 85 % among the different weight percentage binary composites and exhibited a degradation efficiency greater than 85 %. Various parameters, such as catalyst dosage, pH, and dye concentration, significantly affected the composites' photocatalytic performance. The prepared binary composites were also effective in sensing trinitrophenol by quenching fluorescence via inner filter effect. These results underline the usage of cost-effective, easily attainable carbon dot-modified cerium tungstate binary nanocomposites as efficient sensors and photocatalysts for the degradation of pollutants.

ce:section.ce:para: Toxic organic dyes from multiple sources released into the environment pose a severe threat to ecosystems and living organisms, as surface and groundwater pollution from these harmful substances disrupts both animal and human life. Complex assembly, as well as the high chemical stability of these dyes, retard natural degradation. Methylene blue is an organic dye that has various uses in different fields, such as the textile industry, aquaculture, laboratory, and medicine. However, after its predetermined use, direct discharge of methylene blue without treatment would contaminate natural water resources that may cause adverse effects on the ecosystem, including neurological, cardiovascular, and gastrointestinal issues to humans and other organisms. Additionally, it can cause skin discoloration, eye burns, mutations, respiratory difficulties, cancer, and allergic dermatitis [1]. Several strategies like adsorption, biological treatments, electro-oxidation, and photocatalysis are utilised to eradicate these pollutants from water [2–5]. Among them, photocatalysis is the most economical, effective, and sustainable method. Hence, conscious concern is substantial in removing these dyes by developing efficient photocatalysts that could convert them into harmless products.

Semiconductor materials like ZnO, TiO2, SnO2, etc., have been used as photocatalysts for methylene blue degradation [6,7]. Yet, some drawbacks, such as faster recombination of active species and expansive band gap energy, diminish their practical applications. These limitations can be nullified using n-type semiconductors such as cerium tungstate in photocatalytic applications. It has a suitable band gap for absorbing visible and UV light, which is essential for generating electron-hole pairs. It is chemically and thermally stable and corrosion-resistant, ensuring long-term usability. The multiple oxidation states of cerium (Ce³⁺/Ce⁴⁺) facilitate redox reactions, enhancing photocatalytic efficiency. The high oxygen storage capacity of Ce3 + and Ce4+ can enhance photocatalytic potential. Furthermore, the ability of Ce3+ to oxidize to Ce4+ states increases oxygen mobility, thereby improving catalytic performance. Efficient charge carrier dynamics, with reduced recombination rates of electron-hole pairs, further enhance its photocatalytic performance. [8,9]. Its large surface area and good adsorption capacity also provide ample active sites for reactions. Also, its various nanostructured forms, such as nanoparticles and nanorods, as well as the modified forms, offer improved surface area and light absorption, making cerium tungstate highly effective in pollutant degradation applications [10,11].

Carbon dots (CDs) are biocompatible, low-cost, zero-dimensional nanomaterials that can be incorporated into semiconductor materials [12]. They are considered excellent and safe candidates for photodegradation owing to their optical stability, high quantum yield, and broad absorption of the solar spectrum [13]. Nowadays, photocatalyst composites have been modified by incorporating CDs, thereby coupling the properties of both composite parent matrix and CDs. CDs' upconverted photoluminescence effect, photosensitization, and outstanding photo-induced charge carrier transfer properties make them suitable fillers for many composite preparations [14]. Creating heterojunctions is an efficient strategy to enhance photocatalytic capacity, as it promotes the separation and movement of photogenerated charge carriers [15]. This effective charge separation suppresses recombination, thereby improving the photodegradation efficiency of the conjugated catalyst [16]. Furthermore, CDs can successfully act as electron harvesters and converters in the catalytic system, and as a result, the band gap energy of most semiconductor photocatalysts modified with CDs is reduced [17]. Similarly, the electron transfer step is crucial in photodegradation due to the significant redox potential difference between electron donor and acceptor species. This difference can be minimized by using a green catalyst, which promotes rapid electron transfer.

In the present work, we have modified cerium tungstate (CT) by carbon dots to synthesize a novel binary composite (CDCT) via hydrothermal treatment followed by ultrasonication. Various amounts of CDs, like 5 %(CT5CD), 10 %(CT10CD), 20 %(CT20CD), and 30 %(CT30CD), were added to cerium tungstate to obtain the different binary composites. As natural precursors, rich in carbon content, play a crucial role in the synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots, we have selected jackfruit peel, an abundant agricultural waste, as the source of carbon in this work [18]. Successful integration of CDs into CT structure could improve the number of active reaction carriers in the system by assisting effective electron transfer. The synthesized binary composite is utilised for the degradation of methylene blue and fluorescent sensing of trinitrophenol.

Cerium (III) nitrate hexahydrate Ce(NO3)3. 6 H2O (Himedia), sodium tungstate Na2WO4. 6 H2O (Merck) were used as precursors. Requisite solutions of cerium nitrate and sodium tungstate were prepared and homogenised under constant stirring for about 1 hour, maintaining the molar ratio at 1:1. The yellowish suspension formed after stirring for 30 minutes was transferred to an autoclave kept at 160 0C for 3 hours. The precipitate formed was washed in deionized water to remove unreacted reagents or byproducts. The product was then dried at 60 0C for 3 hours in a hot air oven and then calcinated at 500 0C for 3 hours in a muffle furnace. Pale yellow-coloured nanocrystals of cerium tungstate were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots from Jackfruit peel
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation method for carbon dots is described in our previous publication [17]. About 5 g of locally collected jackfruit peel was finely powdered and dispersed in 50 ml of deionized water, followed by heating in an autoclave for 12 hours at 180 0C. The dispersion was filtered through Whatman filter paper and washed with dichloromethane by centrifuging for 15 minutes to remove the impurities. The carbon dots were extracted from the aqueous layer by centrifuging three times for 20 minutes at 12,000 rpm and were kept at 4 0C as a solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dot incorporated cerium tungstate
ce:section.ce:para: CD/ CT was prepared by a facile method in which four different amounts of CDs (5 %, 10 %, 20 %, and 30 %) were added to cerium tungstate suspension by ultrasonication. The dispersed product was air-dried and calcined at 300 0C for 3 hours. They are designated as CT5CD, CT10CD, CT20CD, and CT30CD. The experimental procedure of the synthesis is summarised in Scheme 1.

The X-ray diffraction patterns were obtained with a Bruker D8 advance instrument over a 2θ range from 10° to 70° under a scan rate of 0.050/s. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were recorded using a Thermo Nicolet iS50 spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscope (HRTEM: Jeol/JEM 2100) observed surface morphology and nanostructure. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) and Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectral studies were carried out by Jeol 6390LA/Oxford XMX N scanning electron microscope. Photoluminescence (PL) was examined on a Shimadzu spectrofluorophotometer using a xenon lamp as an excitation source at room temperature. The Raman spectrum was performed by using the WITec Alpha 300 RA instrument. X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific ESCALAB Xi+) was used to study the chemical state and electronic structure. Photodegradation studies were conducted using Perkin Elmer lambda 365 UV Vis spectrophotometer.

In a typical method, known concentrations and volumes of the composite and the analytes were sonicated for 4 minutes at pH 5. Organic compounds such as toluene, ethanol, uric acid, 2-nitrophenol (2NP), phenol, trinitrophenol (TNP), 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) and nitrobenzene were added to CD at a pH 5, and fluorescence spectra were recorded for selectivity studies. Sensitivity towards trinitrophenol was studied in a concentration range of 0–1100 µM. Similarly, interference studies were also conducted.

Photocatalytic activity of the synthesized samples was studied by the removal of methylene blue. In the present study, various parameters such as concentration of dye, catalyst dosage and pH were altered. In each test, a known quantity of the photocatalyst was added to the dye solution, stirred and kept dark for 30 minutes. Samples were retrieved at specific intervals of time and UV spectra were recorded.

Crystal structures and crystallite sizes of the pure cerium tungstate and CDs with various contents of CDs were examined by X-ray diffraction (XRD), and the results are depicted in Fig. 1. All peaks can be indexed to the monoclinic phase Ce2(WO4)3 scheelite structure with a space group C2/c. The diffraction peaks at 2θ= 18.2, 28.7, 30.4, 33.6, 39.3, 46.4, 47.7, 52.9, 56.4 and 58.0 correspond to the (111), (023), (040), (204), (242), (060), (115), (431), (442) and (406) Miller indices planes of Ce2(WO4)3 and is in good consistence with JCPDS card no. 31–0340. The absence of impurity peaks demonstrates the high purity of the sample, whereas sharp diffraction peaks verify the crystallinity of the samples. CDs are found to be amorphous, and hence, diffraction peaks are not reflected in the composites due to the low concentration of CDs. Also, intensities of all peaks decline as the amount of doped CDs increases, suggesting slight variation in crystal orientation on doping. The average crystallite size calculated by the Debye-Scherrer equation is 42.8 nm.

The FTIR spectroscopy was used to determine the functional groups in the as-prepared CT, CD and all composites ( Fig. 2a). O-W-O symmetric stretching vibrations are confirmed by the peak at 934 cm−1 and peaks in the range of 725–840 cm−1 corresponds to the W-O-W stretching vibrations [19]. The bands at 1416 and 1625 cm−1 are linked with the bending mode of O-H group. The band at 1556 cm−1 in CTCD represents N-H group while the one at 1390 cm−1 indicates the presence of CC bond formed by doping with carbon dots. Fig. 2b portrays the surface microstructural difference of pure cerium tungstate and the as prepared hybrid samples studied by Raman spectroscopy. Various peaks between 600 cm−1 and 1000 cm−1 in the spectra denote the stretching of the W-O band. Raman spectrum of CT displays intense band at 925 cm−1 corresponding to the symmetric stretching of W-O and band at 806 cm−1 denotes the Eg vibration modes of W-O. For composites, the D band and G band of carbon at 1362 and 1593 cm−1 can be found which indicates the inclusion of CDs in the hybrid sample [20].

The morphology of the synthesized samples was characterized by FESEM and TEM analysis. Fig. 3a shows the FESEM image of CT, and 3b shows the FESEM image of CT10CD. It is seen that the hybrid material has a spherical morphology, and several nanosheets can be seen among spherical hierarchical assemblies. Spherical structures formed by intercrossing these nanoplates provide numerous surface-active sites for photocatalysis and adsorption. CDs are not identified from FESEM images due to their small size. However, EDS elemental mapping confirmed their existence by confirming the presence of Ce, W, O, and C ( Fig. 4).

Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) revealed the elaborate structure of CD, CT, and CT10CD. Fig. 5a shows the TEM image of carbon dots, and Fig. 5b shows the TEM image of the hybrid material. Carbon dots prepared from jackfruit peel had a spherical morphology with an average size of 6.3 nm. In Fig. 5b, we can see many spherical dark spots with diameters around 6 nm, confirming the successful integration of carbon dots in CT. It is a substantial indication by elemental mapping to prove the CDs' anchoring on the CT surface. Fig. 5c shows the SAED pattern of CT10CD, which confirms the crystalline nature of CT10CD in accordance with the XRD pattern.

Fig. 6a depicts the wide X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) of Ce2(WO4)3. In the spectra, main peaks for Ce (3d level), W (4 f level), and O (1 s level) are detected. High-resolution W4f levels (Fig. 6b) display the existence of main components W4f7/2 and W4f5/2 at respectively 34.9 and 37.9 eV and is by the energy found in the literature for W6+ [21]. Ce3d levels consist of two multiplets relating to the occupation of the 4 f level of cerium [22]. The shape of Ce3d (Fig. 6d) appears like Ce3+ because the peak at 916.9 eV representing Ce4+ is not detected [23]. The O1s level (Fig. 6c) produces a main peak at 530.7 eV from bulk lattice oxygen and a minute component at 532.5 eV from oxygen defects [24].

Three distinct zones of weight loss identified during CT thermal breakdown are depicted in Fig. 7. Among these, zone (I), which is between 50 °C and 250 °C, is linked to the dehydration process- weight loss from surface hydration and adsorbed water removal, as well as the removal of other volatile substances like ammonia. Further weight loss occurs in zone (II), between 250 °C and 470 °C, due to organic matter pyrolysis and metal salt breakdown [25]. The chemical is stable in this temperature range, as evidenced by the slight weight shift. The third weight loss is related to the pyrolysis of the organometallic complex, which results in the oxide's final crystallization. It happens across a limited temperature range, between 470 °C and 600 °C. It is possibly related to the loss of oxygen or tungsten oxide causing oxygen defects. The negligible weight change after 600 0C suggests that the final material is stable.

The optical bandgap is a significant parameter that regulates optoelectronic properties. Absorption spectra can identify electronic transitions in a sample. The plot of (αhγ)2 against hγ gives the bandgap in a sample. Fig. 8a shows the diffused reflectance spectra of CT and CT10CD samples. The absorbance values against wavelength are plotted in Fig. 8b. In the ultraviolet region below 400 nm, a broad absorption band with different peaks is observed. 4f-5d transition in Ce3+ ions resulted in an absorption peak at 342 nm, while a small peak at 300 nm may be caused by the charge transfer transition from 2p orbital of oxygen to 4 f orbital of cerium [26]. The absorption band of WO4 2- ion is obtained at 262 nm when oxygen 2p electron moves into any empty tungsten 5d orbitals [27].

On using the Tauc plot to find out the optical bandgap, 2.5 eV, 2.59 eV, 2.71 eV, 2.86 eV, and 3.21 eV were found to be the bandgaps for CT30CD, CT20CD, CT10CD, CT5CD, and CT respectively. The bandgap decreased with the incorporation of CD. The interaction of electrons in f orbital with conduction and valence band results in bandgap in the case of cerium tungstate. However, in doped samples, the occurrence of defects, intrinsic surface states, and local bond distortion may result in a reduction in the bandgap.

Fig. 9 shows the photoluminescence spectra when CT is excited at 370 nm (Ce3+ band) [28]. A broad emission spectrum ranging from 400 nm to 570 nm with a peak maximum of approximately 460 nm was detected. The broad band emission can suggest a multi-phonon or multi-level process [29]. The excitation from the ground state (1A1) of WO4 2− led to four excited states: 1T2, 1T1, 3T2, and 3T1. Among them, only the transition between 1A1 and 1T2 is electric dipole allowed [30]. But, all the transitions between the ground state 1A1 and excited states 1T2, 1T1, 3T2, and 3T1 of tetrahedral WO4 2− ion turn out to be partially allowed owing to the impact of Jahn– Teller effect and is evident from the photoluminescence spectrum.

Photoluminescence spectra are usually used to study the efficiency of charge carrier trapping and energy transfer mechanisms. The samples produce the broad emission between 400 and 570 nm relaxed back into the tungstate ground state after being excited into the tungstate 1T2 band at 370 nm. Radiative broad emissions can be produced from each band where the excited state decays non-radiatively to the 1T1, 3T2, and 3T1 bands. The 1T2 band's energy can be used to pump the Ce3+ ion's 5D5/2 and 5D3/2 ground states before relaxing to the 2F ground states 2F7/2 and 2F5/2 that, resulting in broad emission [31]. Energy can be transferred to both the 1T1 band of the WO4 2− anion complex and the 5D3∕2 state of Ce3+ when the samples are excited into the 5D5∕2 state. Energy can be relaxed back into the ground state from these states, resulting in a broad emission in the 400–570 nm region. As a result, the combination of Ce3+ and WO4 2− combining many energy levels inside the bandgap may cause the observed luminescence feature. [32]. The oxygen vacancies and surface states also contribute to the intensity of photoluminescence. The Jahn-Teller effect is also proposed to cause the PL spectra's triple peak or spread-eagle form. [33]. The deconvoluted spectrum of cerium tungstate is displayed in Fig. 9a. At 427, 460, and 495 nm, the photoluminescence emission spectra can be deconvoluted into three Gaussian peaks. These peaks are assumed to represent the corresponding emissions from the 1T1, 3T2, and 3T1 band relaxations to the 1A1 ground state. [34].

Fig. 9b represents the deconvoluted photoluminescence spectrum of CT10CD. PL intensity is significantly enhanced when carbon dot is doped into cerium tungstate. Among all the hybrid composites, CT10CD shows the maximum PL intensity at an excitation wavelength of 400 nm. In addition to the increase in intensity, a red shift was also observed when compared with the bare sample. The deconvoluted emission spectrum displayed three Gaussian peaks at 450 nm, 480 nm, and 520 nm, representing relaxations to the ground state. The rise in PL intensity may be attributed to several reasons: the quantum confinement effect, surface passivation, defect states, aggregation-induced emission, and dielectric environment. Quantum confinement results from the small size of carbon dots, usually less than 10 nm. The energy levels become distinct due to this process, increasing the bandgap and emission wavelengths. Also, surface passivation by various functional groups may alter the electronic structure. Furthermore, the defect states, such as oxygen-containing functional groups, may introduce new energy levels, eventually increasing the emission wavelength. Similarly, aggregation of carbon dots in the nanocomposite will result in interparticle interactions and a red shift in emission wavelength. Hence, it is evident that the incorporating carbon dots in cerium tungstate can tailor the PL emission properties. Cerium tungstate can emit radiations typically around 420–500 nm and are found to be relatively weak. PL emission peak shifts to 450–530 nm with significant enhancement in intensity due to the synergic effect of Ce3+ ions and CDs in the case of hybrid composites. Also, CDs act as sensitizers, transferring energy to Ce3+ ions, resulting in increased PL intensity. Similarly, CDs can passivate surface defects in cerium tungstate, leading to improved stability and reduced photobleaching.

The feasibility of employing CT10CD to detect trinitrophenol (TNP) was explored through a solution-state fluorescence quenching methodology. The CT10CD exhibited strong emission in the absence of TNP, and sudden fluorescence quenching within a short contact time was observed when TNP was added. Also, I0/I and concentration of TNP showed a linear relationship (R2= 0.9917) where the concentration of TNP ranges from 0 to 1000 μL. Here, I0 and I represent the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of TNP, respectively. The detection limit was 0.884 μM using the equation LOD= 3σ/k where σ is the standard deviation and k is the slope. Furthermore, during the interference studies, it was found that the efficiency of CT10CD in detecting TNP was not affected. The results are presented in Fig. 10.

Absorption spectrum of TNP was analysed to verify the mechanism of sensing. There is a clear spectral overlap for TNP with that of CT10CD. Static quenching can be confirmed as no new absorption peak was identified for the mixture of CT10CD and TNP. Inner filter effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) may be possible mechanisms. For an IFE mechanism, it is essential to have a suitable quencher-fluorophore pair. It will be efficient only if there is a suitable spectral overlap between the absorption band of the quencher and the excitation and/or emission bands of the fluorophore [35]. Here, the absorption bands of the quencher overlap with the excitation band of the fluorophore. This allowed the absorption of light by TNP emitted by the fluorophore, CT10CD, which results in fluorescence quenching. Since the excitation spectrum of CT10CD and the absorption spectrum of TNP displayed good spectral overlap, a blend of static quenching with the IFE mechanism confirms the excellent selectivity of CT10CD for TNP to act as a potential fluorescent sensor. To confirm the sensing mechanism, zeta potential as well as fluorescence lifetime were analysed. No fluorescence resonance energy transfer was identified by studying the fluorescence lifetime of CT10CD before and after the addition of TNP. The average fluorescence lifetime of CT10CD was calculated to be 3.12 ns with χ2 value of 1.19, which changes to 3.06 ns with a χ2 value of 1.11 with the addition of TNP. As there is no significant change in the lifetime before and after the addition of TNP, it can be deduced that IFE is the reason for the turn-off in fluorescence. Additionally, the spectral overlap between the absorption and excitation spectra confirms the possibility of IFE during the sensing of TNP. From zeta potential measurement, it is evident that the surface of CT10CDs was negatively charged (-26.1 mV), and the interaction between trinitrophenol and the negatively charged surface of CT10CDs may be the reason for sensing applications. The reduced negative zeta potential (-16.6 mV) signifies a reduction in the electrostatic repulsion, allowing for closer proximity between particles and enhanced fluorescence quenching. A brief comparison between the present study and the recent reports is shown in Table 1.

To determine the photocatalytic capacity of the CTCD composite, the degradation of MB solution under UV irradiation was done at room temperature. It was estimated by detecting the change in UV–vis absorption with time. When comparing the photodegradation results, a significant increase in photoactivity was observed for the CT10CD nanocomposite compared to pure CT, pure CD, and other composites. These findings indicate that combining CT and CD enhances the catalyst’s ability to absorb ultraviolet light. Fig. 11(a-f) gives the relationship between light absorbance and irradiation time of UV light for various nanocomposites over 120 minutes of UV exposure. Inset of Fig. 11d shows colour of MB solution before degradation and after degradation at an irradiation time of 120 minutes. It is seen that degradation was negligible without a catalyst under UV irradiation. Also, under identical experimental conditions, no noticeable changes were observed in the UV-Vis absorption of the MB solution. This proves that the catalyst is essential for photodegradation under UV-light irradiation. Methylene blue dye exhibits a major absorption band at 664 nm, gradually decreasing with increased UV exposure time for the CT10CD nanocomposite. In contrast, the degradation efficiency was lower for pure CT, CD, and other composites. After 120 minutes of UV irradiation, the CT10CD nanocomposite achieved 88 % degradation of methylene blue, whereas pure CD achieved 70 % and pure CT 63 %. All nanocomposites degraded more than 75 % of the dye, and the results indicate that the composites display remarkably much more significant activity than bare CT and CD in the degradation of MB. The synergic effect of CT and CD may be the reason for this high photocatalytic efficiency. The combination of CT and CD likely increases photogenerated electrons and reduces electron-hole pair recombination, which may be the reason for the good degradation efficiency of nanocomposites [41]. Greater UV-light absorption capacity and availability of optically active centres enhance the degradation reaction. The increased light absorption of CDs facilitates the formation of electron-hole pairs. The functional groups on CDs act as electron scavengers, suppressing electron-hole recombination, eventually resulting in effective charge transfer [42]. The high density of separated holes favours the generation of active radicals essential for photodegradation [43]. Also, functional groups on the surface can form effective H–bonding interactions with the dye as they act as active sites for photocatalysis. Additionally, surface-adsorbed dye and CDs could indulge in fast electron transfer, improving the separation of electron-hole pairs. Hence, this method of water purification treatment can be considered cost-effective and a promising methodology.

The photodegradation mechanism can be explained by considering the activity of both CT and CD in the composite. The activation of the photocatalyst by surface reformation under electromagnetic radiation irradiation is the initial step of photocatalysis. Electrons are excited into higher states by electromagnetic radiations through interaction with the surface plasmons of the photocatalyst. The positively charged hole is produced in place of the negatively charged electron upon excitation. These electron-hole pairs are the reason for the photocatalytic action. The energy needed to activate this action is equivalent to the band gap. The electromagnetic radiation from the incident source gives this necessary energy. In the case of semiconductor catalysts, light irradiation creates photogenerated electron-hole pairs, and in the present work, CD plays a significant role in photocatalysis [44]. Degradation of MB dye occurs due to electron transfer and generation of active hydroxyl radicals, as shown in Scheme 2. The combination of CT and CD results in a complex structure offering access to photo-induced charge transfer transitions upon the irradiation of UV light [45]. The transfer of a photo-induced electron to joint charge transfer states, which are primarily found at the CDs, is caused by this complicated structure. The hole remains electronically and structurally close to CT, which helps prevent the recombination of electron-hole pairs. This, in turn, enhances the reactivity of the photogenerated electrons and holes when exposed to UV light. The electrons in the conductive network of CDs interact with adsorbed O₂, forming oxygen radicals (O₂•⁻). Meanwhile, the upconversion optical properties of CDs enable the conversion of long-wavelength light into short-wavelength light, which subsequently excites CT, generating electron-hole pairs [46]. Superoxide radicals are created when the generated electron combines with the oxygen in the atmosphere. By allowing the hydroxyl ions to interact with the holes, the presence of H2O further promotes the photocatalytic reaction and causes the formation of hydroxyl free radicals. Through their interactions with the MB, these non-selective and reactive radicals cause demethylation, which breaks the N-CH3 bond. This is then succeeded by the radical species attacking the aromatic ring. As a result, the aromatic ring of MB breaks, producing most of the reaction intermediates. The dye fragments break down into further chemical intermediates such as aldehydes, amines, carboxylic acids, and phenols, which are then transformed into comparatively harmless products like H2O and CO2. During the degradation of MB dye, the π–π* interaction between the conjugated structure of CDs and MB facilitates the adsorption of MB on the CTCD composite surface [47]. Based on previous studies, the key photocatalytic reaction steps have been outlined. These synergistic effects collectively contribute to the enhanced photocatalytic performance of the CTCD composite compared to bare CT. The decreased absorbance value denotes MB degradation, and the process follows a pseudo first order with a rate of 0.00844 min-1. The graphs of MB yielded almost straight lines following the pseudo-first-order kinetic model where C 0 and C are the concentration of MB before and after light irradiation, k is the pseudo-first-order rate constant, and t is irradiation time. This explains the potential of the material to act as an efficient photocatalyst for the degradation of MB. A brief comparison between the present study and the recent reports is shown in Table 2.
(1) CT + hν → CT (e- (CB) + h+ (VB)) → CDs(e-) + h+
(2) CT(e-) + O2 → O2 - + CT + H2O→ .OH + CT
(3) CT(h+) + OH- → .OH + CT
(4) CDs(e-) + O2 → O2 - + CDs + H2O → .OH + CDs
(5)
.OH + O2 . -+ h+ (VB) +MB dye → degraded dye
(6)
.OH + O2 . -+ h+ (VB) + degraded dye → CO2 ↑ + H2O

Methylene blue (MB) solution of initial concentration 5 mg/L was degraded with CT alone and the composites with and /or without UV–vis irradiation. Not much degradation could be obtained in the absence of UV-Vis radiation. Both CT and the composites could achieve better MB degradation (40 and 85 %) after 1 hour irradiation, respectively. This indicates the significance of UV-Vis radiation in the photodegradation of MB by the composites.

The effect of different reaction variables was studied, taking CT10CD as the reference catalyst. The initial concentration of dye solution was varied from 5 and 8 mg/L in the presence of 0.02 g photocatalyst under UV light irradiation to study the effect of dye concentration. From Fig. 13a, it is evident that the degradation efficiency of the dye is maximum when the dye concentration was 5 mg/L at 240 minutes of irradiation. Due to the increased availability of more active sites for MB molecules to be adsorbed on the surface of the photocatalyst (CT10CD), the adsorption capacity is higher at lower concentrations. The number of dye molecules on the surface of the photocatalyst increases with an increase in dye concentration, and there is also the probability of dye molecules harvesting light, preventing the photons from reaching the photocatalyst. These may be the reasons for the low efficiency of the photocatalyst for dye removal at higher concentrations of dye solution.

Photocatalyst dosage was altered from 0.005 to 0.03 g to study the degradation percentage of MB under UV light irradiation. From Fig. 12b, it is clear that the photodegradation rate increases with an increase in catalyst dosage from 0.005 g to 0.02 g due to more active sites. However, the activity decreases when the catalyst concentration is increased to 0.03 g. Two reasons may rationalize the decrease in activity beyond 0.02 g. It may be due to the penetration of photoactivating light into the reaction system and the reduction of active sites, thereby reducing the availability on the catalyst surface. Also, activated molecules may get deactivated by collision with the catalyst in the ground state during higher catalyst loading.

Another significant parameter in MB degradation is the solution's pH variation. A change in pH can affect the surface charge of the catalyst. To investigate the influence of solution pH on photocatalytic degradation, dye concentration, and catalyst concentration were taken to be 5 mg/L and 0.02 g, respectively. Solution pH was varied between 3 and 11 by adding HCl or NaOH (1 M). It was found that there was rapid degradation as the contact time increased, and the rate increased when the pH changed from 3 to 9. But there was no significant change for further increase in pH after 9. The degradation percentage increases from 20 % at pH= 3–88 % at pH= 9 for the composite.

In addition to analysing dye degradation using UV–vis absorption spectra, HPLC was conducted to verify the process further. When the degradation of methylene blue was examined before and after catalyst addition, the results corresponded with dye decolouration and UV–vis absorbance findings. Fig. 14 illustrates the initial and final peaks, showing a significant reduction in the intensity of the initial peak post-degradation. The methylene blue peak appeared at a retention time (Rt) of 5.6 minutes, while after degradation, lower-intensity peaks emerged at retention times of 5.1 and 3.4 minutes. These peaks are assumed to represent degradation products, likely differing in structure and concentration from the original sample [54].

As stability and reusability are the most notable features of a photocatalyst, they were investigated towards methylene blue removal ( Fig. 15). To confirm the stability, XRD and FT-IR spectra were analyzed before and after using the photocatalyst. The XRD pattern of the fresh and used catalysts is shown in Fig. 15a and is found to be almost identical. Similarly, there is consistency in the FT-IR spectra of fresh and used photocatalysts, as shown in Fig. 15b. Hence, it can be concluded that the structural features are intact during photocatalytic degradation. Recyclability was investigated by conducting the degradation process for five consecutive cycles, and degradation tendency was studied. The C/C0 graph remained almost constant, and even after five runs, the photocatalytic activity declined only nearly 17 %, which displays the stability of the photocatalyst (Fig. 15c). These results led to the industrial applications of the catalyst against environmental pollutants.

In summary, we prepared cerium tungstate nanoparticles by hydrothermal synthesis and ultrasonicated it with carbon dots to achieve better optical properties. XRD confirmed crystallinity, while FTIR and Raman spectra verified the presence of CDs, identifying the functional groups and the distinct D and G bands. TEM and FESEM images revealed the morphology, highlighting the sheet-like structure of cerium tungstate and the spherical nature of carbon dots. The optical characteristics suggest that composite formation leads to reduced band gap, enhancing its potential for photochemical processes. The samples were utilized for both methylene blue photodegradation and the detection of organic pollutants. Fluorescence quenching via the inner filter effect enabled highly sensitive and selective detection of trinitrophenol, achieving a detection limit of 0.884 μM. Furthermore, the addition of CT10CD facilitated the degradation of methylene blue by 88 % at pH 9 following pseudo first order kinetics. Thus, the synthesized binary composite effectively removes hazardous organic contaminants from wastewater, reducing their environmental impact.

Tomy Ann Mary: Investigation, Formal analysis. Joseph Kuruvilla: Resources, Methodology. Mathew Beena: Resources, Methodology. Kurian Manju: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Paul Anju: Writing – original draft, Validation, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors Anju Paul and Manju Kurian acknowledge the infrastructural support provided by DST-FIST (Ref No: SR/FST/College- 086/2011) and RUSA (GO (Rt) No 82/2019/ HEdn) facilities of Mar Athanasius College, Kothamangalam.
